skill.name: architectural-analysis
skill.description: Guides an AI assistant through a structured architectural audit for dead code, duplicated logic, type issues, coupling problems, and code smells.
skill.summary: Run a structured architecture review for code health, duplication, and maintainability risks.
skill.author: Cygnusfear

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium indicators, but review found the Ruby execution and weak cryptography alerts are false positives caused by Markdown examples and prose. The skill has legitimate medium risk because it instructs agents to run repository-wide shell searches and write an audit report, with no evidence of malicious intent, prompt injection, network access, credential access, or data exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Wide Shell Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to use shell commands such as find, grep, wc, sort, and head across the target repository. This is legitimate for architecture analysis, but it can enumerate broad filesystem contents and should run only in trusted workspaces.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit bash examples used for repository analysis. No user-controlled command construction or hidden execution path was found, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Traversal and Audit Report Output
medium_findings.description: The skill directs the agent to inspect all source files and create a report under .audits. This requires filesystem read access and limited write access, which is expected for the workflow but should be disclosed to users.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly require broad file analysis and generation of a report file. The same file also states not to edit project files, which reduces but does not remove filesystem risk.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Reported as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many Ruby backtick execution findings. The reviewed locations are Markdown code fences and inline code examples inside SKILL.md, not executable Ruby source code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md is a Markdown instruction file, and the flagged backticks delimit examples or inline literals. No Ruby file, dynamic execution primitive, or executable script was present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography findings map to ordinary prose in the description and success criteria. No hashing, encryption, signing, or cryptographic API usage was found in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines contain descriptive text and checklist items, not cryptographic code. There is no supporting evidence for a real weak algorithm issue.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hidden File Access Is Git Directory Exclusion
low_findings.description: The hidden file access finding points to a find command that excludes .git directories from traversal. This reduces scanning scope and does not access hidden files for extraction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command uses -not -path to skip .git paths. The semantic context is exclusion, not reading or exfiltrating hidden files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository-Wide Shell Command Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs the agent to use shell commands such as find, grep, wc, sort, and head across the target repository. This is legitimate for architecture analysis, but it can enumerate broad filesystem contents and should run only in trusted workspaces.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit bash examples used for repository analysis. No user-controlled command construction or hidden execution path was found, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Traversal and Audit Report Output
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs the agent to inspect all source files and create a report under .audits. This requires filesystem read access and limited write access, which is expected for the workflow but should be disclosed to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly require broad file analysis and generation of a report file. The same file also states not to edit project files, which reduces but does not remove filesystem risk.

content.value_statement: Large codebases accumulate dead code, duplicate logic, and unclear module boundaries. This skill gives Claude, Codex, or Claude Code a structured workflow for finding maintainability risks and reporting cleanup priorities.
actual_capabilities: Maps repository structure and identifies entry points before analysis.
Checks exports, imports, and references to classify dead or possibly dead code.
Guides duplicate logic discovery across functions, types, utilities, and components.
Reviews architectural issues such as god objects, circular dependencies, coupling, and layer violations.
Searches for TypeScript type abuse, unsafe assertions, ignored errors, and missing types.
Produces a structured architectural report with impact estimates and cleanup recommendations.
limitations: It is an analysis workflow and does not automatically refactor or delete code.
Dynamic imports, reflection, framework conventions, and generated code can require manual verification.
Results depend on repository access, language mix, and the assistant following each phase carefully.
The skill uses shell search examples, so users should run it only in trusted workspaces.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Legacy Cleanup Plan
use_cases.description: Find dead files, unused exports, and duplicated utilities before a modernization sprint.

use_cases.title: Review a TypeScript Application
use_cases.description: Inspect type safety, repeated interfaces, unsafe casts, and ignored compiler errors.

use_cases.title: Assess Architecture Before Refactoring
use_cases.description: Identify coupling, layer violations, circular dependencies, and oversized modules before making structural changes.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Architecture Audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Start a general review of a repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the architectural-analysis skill to review this repository. Focus on dead code, duplicate functionality, architecture problems, type issues, and code smells.

prompt_templates.title: Dead Code Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the architectural-analysis skill and prioritize dead code detection. Check exports, imports, tests, dynamic usage, and public API boundaries before recommending removal.

prompt_templates.title: Duplication and Type Audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Look for inconsistent implementations and type drift.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the architectural-analysis skill to find duplicated logic, duplicated TypeScript types, unsafe any usage, type assertions, and ignored compiler errors. Group related findings by impact.

prompt_templates.title: Refactoring Readiness Assessment
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a detailed plan before a major refactor.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the architectural-analysis skill for a complete audit. Include dead code, duplication, coupling, layer violations, circular dependencies, type safety issues, code smells, estimated cleanup size, and a prioritized remediation plan.

output_examples.input: Review this TypeScript repository for dead code.
output: A dead code section listing unused files, unused exports, and possibly dead symbols.
Confidence levels for each removal candidate.
Notes about tests, public APIs, and dynamic references that require verification.

output_examples.input: Find duplicated functionality before we refactor services.
output: A grouped duplication report by exact, similar, conceptual, and type duplication.
Affected files, impact, and a recommendation for which implementation to keep.
A cleanup estimate for duplicated lines and affected modules.

output_examples.input: Assess architecture health for a legacy app.
output: An executive summary of dead code, duplication, coupling, type issues, and code smells.
Specific architectural anti-patterns such as god objects and layer violations.
A prioritized remediation plan with risk areas and expected maintainability gains.

best_practices: Run the audit from the repository root so file discovery and import searches are complete.
Review possibly dead items with product and framework context before deleting code.
Treat the report as a refactoring plan and make cleanup changes in small, tested batches.
anti_patterns: Do not delete code solely because one search command found no references.
Do not run the skill in repositories that contain secrets unless the workspace is trusted.
Do not mix architectural analysis with unrelated feature changes in the same review.

faq.question: Does this skill change my code?
faq.answer: No. The skill instructs the assistant to analyze code and produce a report, not modify project files.

faq.question: Which languages does it fit best?
faq.answer: The examples focus on TypeScript and JavaScript, but the review concepts apply to many codebases.

faq.question: Can it prove code is unused?
faq.answer: It can build strong evidence, but dynamic references and public APIs may still need human verification.

faq.question: Why does it use shell commands?
faq.answer: It uses commands such as find and grep to inspect file structure, references, and repeated patterns.

faq.question: Is it safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use it only in trusted workspaces because it reads repository contents and may write an audit report.

faq.question: What output should I expect?
faq.answer: Expect an architectural report with sections for dead code, duplication, anti-patterns, type issues, code smells, and cleanup impact.
